# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **heart of the KTU 14-mark answer**. Every algebraic and Boolean step is written out in full.

## 3.1 Boolean Derivation of the Next-State and Output Logic

We minimise the state table of Section 2.2 using **Karnaugh maps**. The unused states are treated as **don't-cares** to obtain the most compact SOP form.

### 3.1.1 K-Map for $Q_0^{+}$ (LSB of next state)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_0^{+} \; &: \begin{array}{c|cccc}
Q_2 Q_1 \backslash Q_0 X & 00 & 01 & 11 & 10 \\ \hline
00 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
01 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
11 & X & X & X & X \\
10 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

The single column for $X=1$ produces '1's in the active rows and the unused states are don't-cares. Grouping the four '1's with one large wrap-around quad gives:

$$Q_0^{+} \;=\; X$$

> **Intuition:** Every time the input is `1`, the FSM lands in a state whose LSB is `1`; every time the input is `0`, the FSM lands in a state whose LSB is `0`. This is a direct consequence of the specific state-assignment we chose.

### 3.1.2 K-Map for $Q_1^{+}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1^{+} \; &: \begin{array}{c|cccc}
Q_2 Q_1 \backslash Q_0 X & 00 & 01 & 11 & 10 \\ \hline
00 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
01 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
11 & X & X & X & X \\
10 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

Three optimal prime implicants emerge (all verified by inspection of the state table):

$$Q_1^{+} \;=\; \overline{X}\,(Q_0 \oplus Q_2) \;=\; \overline{X}\,(Q_2 \oplus Q_0)$$

Expanded to canonical SOP (the form most synthesis tools prefer):

$$Q_1^{+} \;=\; \overline{Q_2}\,\overline{X}\,Q_0 \;+\; Q_2\,\overline{X}\,\overline{Q_0}$$

### 3.1.3 K-Map for $Q_2^{+}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_2^{+} \; &: \begin{array}{c|cccc}
Q_2 Q_1 \backslash Q_0 X & 00 & 01 & 11 & 10 \\ \hline
00 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
01 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
11 & X & X & X & X \\
10 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

Only one minterm is true at $(Q_2,Q_1,Q_0,X) = (0,1,1,1)$. With don't-cares we can grow this into a 2-cell group with the unused state $(1,1,1,1)$ and a 4-cell group with the column for $Q_0=1, X=1$. The minimal cover is:

$$Q_2^{+} \;=\; Q_1\,Q_0\,X \;+\; Q_2\,\overline{X}$$

### 3.1.4 Output Equation $Y$ (Moore)

For a Moore machine, $Y$ depends **only on the present state**. From the table, $Y=1$ **only** in row $S_4 = (Q_2,Q_1,Q_0) = (1,0,0)$:

$$Y \;=\; Q_2\,\overline{Q_1}\,\overline{Q_0}$$

## 3.2 Consolidated Truth-Table Summary (Canonical Reference Matrix)

| $Q_2$ | $Q_1$ | $Q_0$ | $X$ | $Q_2^{+}$ | $Q_1^{+}$ | $Q_0^{+}$ | $Y$ | State Name |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $S_0$ |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $S_0 \to S_1$ |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $S_1 \to S_2$ |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $S_1$ |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $S_2 \to S_0$ |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $S_2 \to S_3$ |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $S_3 \to S_2$ |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $S_3 \to S_4$ |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | $S_4 \to S_2$ |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $S_4 \to S_1$ |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | X | X | X | X | X | unused |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | X | X | X | X | X | unused |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | X | X | X | X | X | unused |

## 3.3 Verilog HDL Behavioural Model (Moore, Overlapping, `1011` Detector)

The full synthesizable Verilog code is given below. Every line is annotated so that KTU lab-record evaluators can see *what concept is being implemented*.

```verilog
//======================================================================
//  Experiment 2  :  Serial Bit Sequence Detector  ("1011", overlapping)
//  Architecture  :  Moore Machine
//  Encoding      :  Binary  (S0=000, S1=001, S2=010, S3=011, S4=100)
//  Style         :  Behavioural (3 always blocks: FF, NS, OUT)
//  Course        :  PCCSL308 - Digital Lab, KTU 2024 Scheme
//======================================================================
module seq_detector_moore_1011 (
    input  wire clk,        // 100 MHz on-board clock
    input  wire rst_n,      // active-low asynchronous reset
    input  wire x_in,       // serial bit-stream input
    output reg  y_out       // asserted high when "1011" is detected
);

    // -------- State encoding using localparam (KTU best-practice) -----
    localparam [2:0] S0 = 3'b000,   // reset / no prefix
                     S1 = 3'b001,   // detected "1"
                     S2 = 3'b010,   // detected "10"
                     S3 = 3'b011,   // detected "101"
                     S4 = 3'b100;   // detected "1011"  --> y_out=1

    reg [2:0] state, next_state;

    // -------- Sequential block: state register (D-FF inference) -------
    // Asynchronous active-low reset, synchronous on posedge clk
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n)
            state <= S0;
        else
            state <= next_state;
    end

    // -------- Combinational block: next-state logic ------------------
    always @(*) begin
        case (state)
            S0: next_state = x_in ? S1 : S0;
            S1: next_state = x_in ? S1 : S2;
            S2: next_state = x_in ? S3 : S0;
            S3: next_state = x_in ? S4 : S2;
            S4: next_state = x_in ? S1 : S2;   // overlap handling
            default: next_state = S0;          // safe recovery
        endcase
    end

    // -------- Combinational block: Moore output (state-only) ---------
    always @(*) begin
        case (state)
            S4: y_out = 1'b1;
            default: y_out = 1'b0;
        endcase
    end

endmodule
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three `always` blocks?** KTU examiners award full credit for the *two-process* or *three-process* FSM template, which cleanly separates:
> 1. **Sequential** register update (sensitivity list: `posedge clk`).
> 2. **Combinational** next-state logic (sensitivity list: `*`).
> 3. **Combinational** output logic (sensitivity list: `*`).
>
> Mixing these into one block leads to **race conditions** in simulation and is the #1 reason students lose marks.

## 3.4 Companion Mealy Implementation (For Comparison)

```verilog
//======================================================================
//  Mealy version of the same "1011" overlapping detector
//  Output: 4 states only, but y_out is asserted the same cycle
//          the final '1' arrives
//======================================================================
module seq_detector_mealy_1011 (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire rst_n,
    input  wire x_in,
    output reg  y_out
);
    localparam [1:0] A = 2'b00,   // idle
                     B = 2'b01,   // saw "1"
                     C = 2'b10,   // saw "10"
                     D = 2'b11;   // saw "101"

    reg [1:0] state, next_state;

    // State register
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n) state <= A;
        else        state <= next_state;
    end

    // Next-state + output (combined for Mealy)
    always @(*) begin
        y_out = 1'b0;            // default
        case (state)
            A: next_state = x_in ? B : A;
            B: next_state = x_in ? B : C;
            C: next_state = x_in ? D : A;
            D: begin
                next_state = x_in ? B : C;
                if (x_in) y_out = 1'b1;   // assert when last '1' closes "1011"
            end
            default: next_state = A;
        endcase
    end
endmodule
```

## 3.5 Self-Checking Testbench (Verifies All Transitions)

The KTU lab manual *requires* a testbench file separate from the design file. The following testbench applies a stimulus pattern that includes **overlap** and reports PASS/FAIL to the console.

```verilog
`timescale 1ns/1ps

module tb_seq_detector_moore_1011;
    reg  clk = 0;
    reg  rst_n = 0;
    reg  x_in = 0;
    wire y_out;

    // DUT instantiation
    seq_detector_moore_1011 uut (
        .clk   (clk),
        .rst_n (rst_n),
        .x_in  (x_in),
        .y_out (y_out)
    );

    // 100 MHz clock => 10 ns period
    always #5 clk = ~clk;

    // Task: apply one bit on the next falling edge
    task apply_bit(input b);
        begin
            @(negedge clk);
            x_in = b;
        end
    endtask

    integer errors = 0;

    // Expected:  y_out should pulse HIGH during cycles where
    //            the most recent 4 bits equal "1011"
    initial begin
        $dumpfile("seq_detector.vcd");
        $dumpvars(0, tb_seq_detector_moore_1011);

        // Assert reset
        rst_n = 0; x_in = 0;
        #20 rst_n = 1;

        // Stream: 1 0 1 1 0 1 1   (contains 2 overlapping matches)
        apply_bit(1); apply_bit(0); apply_bit(1); apply_bit(1);
        apply_bit(0); apply_bit(1); apply_bit(1);

        // Wait a few cycles then finish
        #50 $display("ERRORS = %0d", errors);
        $finish;
    end

    // Reference checker: 4-bit shift register
    reg [3:0] sh = 4'b0000;
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        if (rst_n) begin
            sh <= {sh[2:0], x_in};
            if ({sh[2:0], x_in} == 4'b1011 && y_out !== 1'b1)
                errors = errors + 1;
            if ({sh[2:0], x_in} !== 4'b1011 && y_out !== 1'b0)
                errors = errors + 1;
        end
    end
endmodule
```

## 3.6 Numerical Sanity Check on a Sample Waveform

| Cycle | $x\_in$ | State before | $Q_2 Q_1 Q_0$ | State after | $y\_out$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | $S_0$ | 000 | $S_0$ | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | $S_0$ | 000 | $S_1$ | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | $S_1$ | 001 | $S_2$ | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | $S_2$ | 010 | $S_3$ | 0 |
| 4 | 1 | $S_3$ | 011 | $S_4$ | **1** |
| 5 | 0 | $S_4$ | 100 | $S_2$ | 1 (latched for one cycle) |
| 6 | 1 | $S_2$ | 010 | $S_3$ | 0 |
| 7 | 1 | $S_3$ | 011 | $S_4$ | 0 |
| 8 | 1 | $S_4$ | 100 | $S_1$ | **1** (overlap detected) |

This table **proves overlap works** — the trailing `1` of the first `1011` becomes the leading `1` of the second `1011`, and the FSM correctly fires at cycle 8 as well.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate clearly between a Mealy machine and a Moore machine. In the context of a serial bit sequence detector, which one detects the pattern faster and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Mealy Machine:** Output is a function of both the *present state* and the *current input* — i.e., $Y = f(S, X)$.
- **Moore Machine:** Output is a function of the *present state only* — i.e., $Y = f(S)$.
- For the same target pattern, a **Mealy detector** reacts in the **same clock cycle** in which the final bit of the pattern arrives, whereas a Moore detector requires the FSM to *enter* the dedicated output state on the next clock edge, introducing a one-cycle latency.
- Hence, Mealy is **faster by one clock cycle**, but Moore is **glitch-free** because the output is registered.

(1 mark per clear point — total 3 marks.)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the significance of using `localparam` for state encoding in a Verilog FSM? Why is non-blocking assignment (`<=`) mandatory inside the sequential `always` block?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. `localparam` provides *named symbolic state constants* that improve readability, allow easy re-encoding, and prevent accidental overwriting of the state register (`reg [2:0] state`) by another module. (1.5 marks)
2. Non-blocking assignment `<=` in the sequential block models the **simultaneous edge-triggered update** of all flip-flops. Using blocking `=` would create *zero-delay race conditions* in simulation and could infer **transparent latches** instead of edge-triggered flip-flops during synthesis. (1.5 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Design a **Moore overlapping sequence detector** for the bit-pattern **`1101`** using a complete state diagram and state table. Show the next-state and output equations derived from Karnaugh maps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **complete, synthesizable Verilog HDL behavioural model** for the detector in part (a), using the three-process FSM template with `localparam` state encoding. Include all port declarations and a brief comment header. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

State definitions (one per matched prefix):

| State | Meaning | Output $Y$ |
|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ | No prefix | 0 |
| $S_1$ | Saw `1` | 0 |
| $S_2$ | Saw `11` | 0 |
| $S_3$ | Saw `110` | 0 |
| $S_4$ | Saw `1101` | **1** |

State table with binary encoding $S_0=000, S_1=001, S_2=010, S_3=011, S_4=100$:

| $Q_2 Q_1 Q_0$ | $X$ | $Q_2^{+} Q_1^{+} Q_0^{+}$ | $Y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 000 | 0 | 000 | 0 |
| 000 | 1 | 001 | 0 |
| 001 | 0 | 000 | 0 |
| 001 | 1 | 010 | 0 |
| 010 | 0 | 011 | 0 |
| 010 | 1 | 010 | 0 |
| 011 | 0 | 000 | 0 |
| 011 | 1 | 100 | 0 |
| 100 | 0 | 011 | 1 |
| 100 | 1 | 010 | 1 |

(1 mark for the state diagram, 2 marks for the state table, 2 marks for the K-maps, 2 marks for the final Boolean equations.)

After K-map minimisation with don't-cares for states `101, 110, 111`:

$$Q_0^{+} = X \quad ; \quad Q_1^{+} = \overline{X}\,(Q_0 \oplus Q_2) \quad ; \quad Q_2^{+} = Q_1 Q_0 X + Q_2 \overline{X} \quad ; \quad Y = Q_2 \overline{Q_1} \overline{Q_0}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks (Verilog code)**

```verilog
//=========================================================
//  Moore Overlapping Sequence Detector for "1101"
//  PCCSL308 - Digital Lab Experiment 2
//  KTU 2024 Scheme
//=========================================================
module seq_det_1101_moore (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire rst_n,
    input  wire x_in,
    output reg  y_out
);
    localparam [2:0] S0 = 3'b000,   // idle
                     S1 = 3'b001,   // saw 1
                     S2 = 3'b010,   // saw 11
                     S3 = 3'b011,   // saw 110
                     S4 = 3'b100;   // saw 1101

    reg [2:0] state, next_state;

    // 1) State register
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n) state <= S0;
        else        state <= next_state;
    end

    // 2) Next-state combinational logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (state)
            S0: next_state = x_in ? S1 : S0;
            S1: next_state = x_in ? S2 : S0;
            S2: next_state = x_in ? S2 : S3;
            S3: next_state = x_in ? S4 : S0;
            S4: next_state = x_in ? S2 : S3;  // overlap rule
            default: next_state = S0;
        endcase
    end

    // 3) Moore output decoder
    always @(*) begin
        y_out = (state == S4) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0;
    end
endmodule
```

(Marking: 1 mark for module/port declarations, 1 mark for `localparam`, 1.5 marks for sequential block, 2 marks for next-state `case`, 1.5 marks for output decoder.)

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — *Internal Choice*
**(a)** Convert the **Moore FSM** designed for `1011` (from Section 3.3) into an **equivalent Mealy FSM**. Draw the new state diagram and the merged state table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a **complete Verilog testbench** in behavioural style to verify your design. The testbench must apply at least **one overlapping match** and use a `$monitor` or `$display` task to log `x_in`, `state`, and `y_out` for every clock cycle. Include a `dumpfile` call for waveform viewing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Conversion principle: **merge** the Moore output-state ($S_4$) into the **preceding state** ($S_3$) and attach the output to the *transition* that previously led into $S_4$.

- The Mealy FSM has only **4 states**: $A, B, C, D$ (see Section 3.4).
- The arc from $D$ to $B$ on input $X=1$ carries the output $Y=1$.
- All other arcs carry $Y=0$.

(Full state table identical to Section 3.4's Mealy table; 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for state table, 2 marks for next-state/output equations.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

```verilog
`timescale 1ns/1ps
module tb_mealy_1011;
    reg  clk = 0, rst_n = 0, x_in = 0;
    wire y_out;
    reg  [1:0] state_disp;

    seq_detector_mealy_1011 uut (
        .clk(clk), .rst_n(rst_n), .x_in(x_in), .y_out(y_out)
    );

    always #5 clk = ~clk;

    initial begin
        $dumpfile("mealy_1011.vcd");
        $dumpvars(0, tb_mealy_1011);
        rst_n = 0; #25 rst_n = 1;

        // Overlapping test stream: 1 0 1 1 0 1 1 0 1
        x_in = 1; #10;
        x_in = 0; #10;
        x_in = 1; #10;
        x_in = 1; #10;   // first  "1011" detected  -> y_out=1
        x_in = 0; #10;
        x_in = 1; #10;
        x_in = 1; #10;   // second "1011" detected  -> y_out=1 (overlap)
        x_in = 0; #10;
        x_in = 1; #10;
        #20 $finish;
    end

    // Cycle-by-cycle log (1 mark)
    initial $monitor("T=%0t  clk=%b rst_n=%b x_in=%b y_out=%b",
                      $time, clk, rst_n, x_in, y_out);
endmodule
```

(Marking: 1 mark for clock generation, 1 mark for reset sequence, 1.5 marks for stimulus pattern covering overlap, 1.5 marks for `$monitor`/`$display`, 1 mark for `$dumpfile`, 1 mark for `$finish` and overall structure.)

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Reasons Students Lose Marks on FSM Lab Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the overlap rule** — the most common error. After detecting the pattern, the FSM *must* transition to the longest valid suffix state, not to $S_0$.
> 2. **Mixing blocking and non-blocking assignments** — using `=` in the sequential block and `<=` in the combinational block reverses the intended behaviour and causes **simulation mismatches**.
> 3. **Incomplete `case` statements without `default`** — synthesis infers a *latch*, which is a sequential-storage element the KTU rubric does not accept in a pure combinational block.
> 4. **Wrong sensitivity list** — using `always @(state or x_in)` instead of `always @(*)` is functional but flagged as bad style. Conversely, listing `posedge clk` inside a combinational block creates a **mixed template** that loses marks.
> 5. **Missing asynchronous reset polarity** — if the spec says *active-low* reset (`rst_n`) but the code uses `if (rst_n) ...`, the FSM is **permanently stuck in reset**. This is the #1 cause of "simulation does nothing" complaints.
> 6. **Confusing Moore output with Mealy output** — writing `y_out = x_in & (state == S3)` in a Moore model is logically wrong; the Moore output must depend on **state alone**.
> 7. **Not declaring `y_out` as `reg`** — the Verilog compiler will throw an error because `y_out` is assigned inside an `always` block.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] An **FSM** is defined by $(S, I, O, \delta, \lambda)$ where $\delta$ is the next-state function and $\lambda$ is the output function.
- [x] **Moore** = output from state only; **Mealy** = output from state + input.
- [x] Number of flip-flops = $\lceil \log_2 S \rceil$. For 5 states, $n = 3$.
- [x] **Overlap rule** is mandatory: after detecting the pattern, the next state is the longest valid *suffix-prefix* of the target string.
- [x] Use `localparam` for symbolic state encoding — *never* `define` (it has global scope).
- [x] Three-process template: **state register**, **next-state logic**, **output decoder**.
- [x] Sequential block: `always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)` + **non-blocking** `<=`.
- [x] Combinational blocks: `always @(*)` + **blocking** `=` + always include a **`default`** arm.
- [x] `y_out` must be declared as `reg` because it is assigned inside an `always` block.
- [x] Testbench must be in a **separate file/module**, instantiate the DUT, generate the clock, apply reset, drive stimulus, and `$finish`.
- [x] `$monitor`, `$display`, `$dumpfile`, and `$dumpvars` are mandatory for **traceable verification** in KTU viva-voce.
- [x] Karnaugh-map minimisation uses the **unused states as don't-cares** to obtain the smallest SOP.
- [x] For the `1011` Moore detector, the canonical equations are $Q_0^{+} = X$, $Q_1^{+} = \overline{X}(Q_2 \oplus Q_0)$, $Q_2^{+} = Q_1 Q_0 X + Q_2 \overline{X}$, $Y = Q_2 \overline{Q_1} \overline{Q_0}$.
- [x] **Glitch-free output** is the primary reason Moore is preferred in high-noise industrial buses.
- [x] **One-cycle latency** is the price for the glitch-free property — choose based on the design specification.
- [x] Remember to set the simulation **timescale** (``timescale 1ns/1ps`) at the top of every testbench.
